courses.architect: C.B. Macdonald (1914); Robert Trent Jones (renovation, 1952); Brian Silva (restoration, 1989-present)
courses.other_details: Ranked #121 in Second 100 Greatest. Has hosted major events like the 1947 U.S. Open and 2014 Curtis Cup.
courses.description: Macdonald's westernmost design, a classic layout known for its undulated terrain and historic prestige. It has been a mainstay on rankings of America’s Second 100 Greatest Courses.
Host of the 1965 U.S. Open and 2018 PGA Championship. Known for its challenging "Green Monster" reputation and Robert Trent Jones Sr. design.
courses.architect: Robert Trent Jones Sr. (1960); Rees Jones (renovation, 2006)
courses.other_details: Ranked in Second 100 Greatest. Hosted the 100th PGA Championship.
Ranked in 100 Greatest Public. Part of the Big Cedar Lodge golf destination.
courses.description: Features ridge-and-valley topography providing a sense of scale. A Bill Coore and Ben Crenshaw masterpiece at the Big Cedar Lodge resort.

courses.other_details: Established in 1960, course opened in 1954. Notable for its testing Robert Trent Jones Sr. layout.
courses.description: A staple on America's 100 Greatest rankings from the late 1960s through much of the 1990s. Hosted the Ryder Cup in 1971.

courses.other_details: Acquired by The Crawford Group in 1991. Known for its private and exclusive nature.
courses.description: Designed by P.B. Dye to emulate his father Pete's architecture without being exaggerated. Set in a scenic valley with two six-acre lakes.

courses.other_details: Opened to outstanding reviews in 2002. Named a Best New Private Course by Golf Digest in 2003.
courses.description: Situated south of St. Louis, it was named the #1 ranked course in Missouri for four consecutive years (2007-2010). Features highland-style holes.

courses.other_details: Named 2021 Best New Public Course in North America. Part of Johnny Morris's Big Cedar Lodge.
courses.description: The first public course designed by Tiger Woods and TGR Design. Named for Missouri native Payne Stewart. Features a dramatic 19th hole "Big Rock".
courses.course_style: Fun and playable for all levels

courses.other_details: Ranked #92 in 100 Greatest Public. Known for its off-season value.
courses.description: The first course built at Big Cedar Lodge, redesigned by Tom Fazio. Features free-ranging buffalo and native grasses.

courses.other_details: Par 71, spans 160 acres. Known for its challenging play around natural water features.
courses.architect: Dr. Michael Hurdzan and Dana Fry (1997)
courses.description: The second course at St. Albans, opened five years after Lewis and Clark. Features 12 holes involving play around creeks, streams, or ponds.
courses.course_name: The Country Club of St. Albans: Tavern Creek

courses.description: Jack Nicklaus signature design tucked along the shorelines of the Lake of the Ozarks. Features a Mediterranean feel.
courses.other_details: Consistently ranked among the top courses in the state since opening in 2000.

courses.description: The first course at the 36-hole St. Albans complex. Commissioned from the renowned duo of Weiskopf and Morrish.
courses.course_name: The Country Club of St. Albans: Lewis and Clark
courses.other_details: Opened in November 1992. Located within a 5,400-acre residential property.
Topped Golfweek's "Best Courses You Can Play" list in Missouri for four years (2012-2015).
courses.description: An exceptional public course honoring Missouri golfers. Incorporates natural rock outcroppings and waterfalls.

courses.other_details: Originally known as The Progress Club. Celebrated 100 years of golf in 2012.
courses.description: Reinvented by Todd Clark and Ron Whitten. A historic club founded in 1881, with the golf course originally opening in 1912.

courses.other_details: Nearly 7,200 yards. Hosted Korn Ferry Tour events (Price Cutter Charity Championship).
courses.description: Built over 600 acres in the Ozarks. Features limestone outcroppings, intense elevation changes, and over 70 bunkers.
Set among dense forests outside St. Louis. Features tight driving areas protected by penal bunkering and challenging greens. Currently undergoing renovation.
courses.other_details: Purchased in 2023 for a major renovation. Features stunning bentgrass putting surfaces.

courses.description: A genuine original, its unique character is forged from the sandy pine barrens of southwest Jersey. Pine Valley has earned the top spot in every edition of America’s 100 Greatest rankings except four times—when it was second each time.
courses.other_details: Ranked No. 1 in America's 100 Greatest Golf Courses.

courses.description: Jack Nicklaus won two U.S. Opens on Baltusrol's Lower Course, setting a tournament record each time. It's a unique masterpiece that has hosted numerous major championships.
courses.other_details: Restored by Gil Hanse. Hosted the 2017 Senior U.S. Open.

courses.description: Somerset Hills is another marvelous A.W. Tillinghast design, one of the few that has remained virtually intact since its opening.
It’s believed that when A.W. Tillinghast began constructing the Upper and Lower Courses at Baltusrol, he intended them to be equal in quality. The Upper course is highly regarded in its own right.
In the late 1990s, Gil Hanse and his team gradually began restoring all of Plainfield’s Donald Ross features, bringing back its original glory.
Ridgewood was always one of A.W. Tillinghast’s favorites. He lived nearby and was a club member. The facility features 27 holes.
Walter Travis designed this course, which continues to move up in the rankings. It is known for its challenging layout and historical significance.
Bayonne Golf Club was a reclamation project, a super-maximalist design that leapfrogged other notable courses in the rankings.
Galloway National occupies a very fine stretch of South Jersey pine barrens, a site that was ideal for a championship course.
Like Bayonne, Liberty National was a reclamation project. It recently dropped out of the top 200 for the first time since its debut.

courses.architect: A.W. Tillinghast, Seth Raynor, Charles Banks
courses.description: Essex County has been touched by some of the game’s best designers. Its work by Charles Banks has just been restored by Gil Hanse.
Mountain Ridge has forever been one of North Jersey's great hidden gems, featuring fabulous Donald Ross designs.
The backbone of this super-maximalist design is a high-budget redo of the old Sand Barrens Golf Course. It features a unique and bold layout.
Located northeast of Princeton, it occupies a great piece of property characteristic of Tom Fazio's designs.

courses.course_name: Trump National Golf Club Bedminster: Old Course
courses.description: The highest-ranked Trump-owned course in the state, it hosted the 2017 U.S. Women's Open.
Big and brawny Arcola Country Club continues to get better year after year.
Set in the northern portion of New Jersey’s Pine Barrens, the course weaves through pine trees on gentle terrain.
Known for minimalist designs with quiet undulation and ridges. Part of the Dormie Network.
Dana Fry designed and built this course in 2001. It underwent significant work in 2022.
courses.architect: Dana Fry and Dr. Michael Hurdzan

courses.description: Governors Club: Lakes & Foothills is ranked as one of the best golf courses in North Carolina. Discover our experts' reviews and tee time information.

Home to the Duke Blue Devils, a top NCAA Division I program, the Duke Golf Club features significant changes in elevation. Originally a 1957 Robert Trent Jones Sr. design, the course underwent a complete reconstruction by his son, Rees Jones, in 1993.
Hope Valley Country Club is one of the best golf courses in North Carolina. Discover our experts' reviews and tee time information.

In the era of social media, it’s rare to find a true hidden gem these days. Sequoyah National might be exactly that—located in the scenic mountains of western North Carolina. Designed by Robert Trent Jones II and opened in 2009, this layout is owned by the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians and offers breathtaking views of the Great Smoky Mountains National Park.

courses.course_name: Greensboro Country Club Farm Course: Farm
courses.description: Greensboro Country Club Farm Course in Greensboro is one of the best courses in North Carolina. Discover our experts' reviews and tee time information.

Formerly a part of Sedgefield Country Club, The Cardinal by Pete Dye is now semi-private. Set along the rolling hills of Greensboro, the course features typical Dye-isms like railroad ties and deep bunkering. Built in 1974 and renovated by Dye in 2006, it remains a challenging test of golf.
courses.course_name: The Cardinal By Pete Dye: Cardinal

courses.description: Trump National Charlotte possesses plenty of aesthetic tee shots and approaches with great views of Lake Norman. The Greg Norman design from 1999 is set on a massive site that feels very private, and is one of the more popular clubs in the Charlotte area.

The Cliffs at Walnut Cove is a Jack Nicklaus design in a valley of the Blue Ridge Mountains and the only private golf club in Asheville. Opened in 2005, it meanders through open meadows and features spectacular mountain vistas.
Bright's Creek in Mill Spring is one of the best courses in North Carolina. Discover our experts’ reviews and tee time information.
Myers Park Country Club is one of the best golf courses in North Carolina. Discover our experts' reviews and tee time information.
Situated on the north side of Durham in the Research Triangle, Treyburn is a private Tom Fazio design that opened in 1988. The course is known for its rolling terrain and challenging green complexes.
Cape Fear Country Club in Wilmington is one of the best courses in North Carolina. Discover our experts’ reviews and tee time information.

Architects are usually only as good as their sites, or at least their budgets—Tom Fazio would certainly attest to that. Mike Strantz was often given extreme sites and Tot Hill Farm is certainly one of them. Opened in 2000, it is known as one of the most difficult and visually stunning courses in the state.

The Tom Fazio-designed Old North State Club is located just over an hour outside of Charlotte, where it is surrounded on three sides by Badin Lake. Opened in 1991, the course is often cited as one of the top Fazio designs in the state.

Arnold Palmer and his designers, Ed Seay and Harrison Minchew, designed an extraordinary mountainous course at Balsam Mountain Preserve. Opened in 2007, the course sits at over 3,000 feet in elevation and offers some of the most dramatic views in North Carolina.

Linville Golf Club was originally founded in 1892 and is one of the oldest clubs in North Carolina. The current Donald Ross course was completed in 1924 and is a quintessential mountain layout that has remained remarkably true to its original design.

Situated at the base of the Blue Ridge Mountains is the Elk River Club, home to Jack Nicklaus’s first North Carolina design. Opened in 1984, the course features a dramatic mountain landscape with the Elk River coming into play on several holes.

courses.course_name: Forest Creek Golf Club: South Course
courses.description: The Tom Fazio-designed South course at Forest Creek Golf Club debuted in 1996 and was followed shortly by the North Course. Both courses are widely considered some of the best in the Pinehurst area and provide a first-class private club experience.

Situated just south of Hendersonville and 45 minutes from Asheville, Tom Fazio devised an 18-hole resort-style course that opened in 1991. The course features significant elevation changes and views of the surrounding mountains.

Opened in 1926, Sedgefield Country Club is a Donald Ross design that has been the longtime host of the PGA Tour's Wyndham Championship. The course underwent a restoration by Kris Spence in 2007 to return it to its original Ross roots.

Cut from a nature preserve about a mile north of the resort, Pinehurst #8 is one of Tom Fazio's most acclaimed designs. Opened in 1996 to celebrate the resort's centennial, the course is known for its elevation changes and lack of residential development.

Debuting in 1948, Raleigh Country Club is one of the few courses that can claim to be the final work of Donald Ross. In 2020, Kyle Franz completed a major renovation that restored the course's classic character and bunkering.

In 2001, Rees Jones designed a golfers’ paradise at Old Chatham Golf Club just outside of Raleigh. The course is set in a pristine pine forest and provides a secluded, traditional golf experience without any homes on the course.

Southern Pines used to be a course that only locals and architectural bookworms played. Designed in 1906 by Donald Ross, the course underwent a major restoration by Kyle Franz in 2021 that has returned it to national prominence.

What began as a private retreat called Knollwood, funded by Roaring Twenties millionaires like James Barber, Mid Pines has remained a beloved Donald Ross classic since opening in 1921. Kyle Franz’s 2013 restoration brought back the sandy, waste-area character that Ross originally intended.

The George Cobb-designed Linville Ridge Golf Course is set among the spectacular Blue Ridge Mountains. At its highest point, the course sits at over 4,900 feet, making it one of the highest elevation courses in the eastern United States.

courses.course_name: The Country Club of North Carolina: Dogwood
courses.description: The Dogwood course at the Country Club of North Carolina sits in a heavily forested region of the North Carolina Sandhills. Originally designed by Ellis Maples and Willard Byrd in 1963, the course has been a frequent site for prestigious amateur tournaments.

Located in the Cashiers region of North Carolina is the George Cobb-designed golf course at High Hampton. Recently renovated by Tom Fazio in 2021, the course is set against the backdrop of Rock Mountain and offers a classic mountain golf experience.

courses.course_name: Forest Creek Golf Club: North Course
courses.description: Tom Fazio did the first 18 at Pinehurst’s ultra-private Forest Creek Golf Club, the South Course, in 1996. He returned in 2002 to complete the North Course, which many members feel is the superior layout due to its more challenging nature and rugged bunkering.

The Dormie Club is a minimalist Coore and Crenshaw design just north of Pinehurst that follows the natural contours of the land. Opened in 2010, the course has transitioned to a private, destination-style club with luxury on-site lodging.

Pine Needles used to lurk quietly in the Pinehurst background before the USGA chose to put it in the spotlight for the U.S. Women's Open (which it has hosted four times). The Donald Ross course, which opened in 1928, was restored by Kyle Franz in 2017.

In 1926, Leonard Tufts, the president of Pinehurst Resort, and some business leaders from nearby Winston-Salem and Greensboro collaborated to create a summer retreat in the Blue Ridge Mountains. They hired Donald Ross to design a course that is now known for its incredible cliff-side finishing holes.

Tobacco Road took every idea that Strantz had been developing to that point (1999) and put it all into one wild, controversial, and ultimately beloved layout. Built in an old sand quarry, the course is known for its blind shots and massive elevation changes.

There was never a dispute about the Donald Ross pedigree of Charlotte Country Club. Ross expanded it to 18 holes in 1915 and then completely redesigned it in 1921. A 2007 restoration by Ron Forse returned the course to its golden age glory.

Situated in the dense forested mountains outside of Asheville is the timeless 1922 Donald Ross-designed Biltmore Forest Country Club. The course and clubhouse are part of a historic district that remains one of the most exclusive enclaves in the state.

Back when Grandfather Golf & Country Club made the 100 Greatest in 2001, we wrote, “This is a Grandfather that hasn't slowed down a bit.” The Elis Maples design from 1968 is widely considered one of the finest mountain courses in the world.

Like a football team searching for the right coach, the resort could never settle on the right identity for No. 4. That changed in 2018 when Gil Hanse and Jim Wagner completely redesigned the course, giving it a rugged, sand-hills character that perfectly complements No. 2.

Sand is the defining character of Pinehurst, and Pinehurst #10 goes right to the source: a former sand quarry. Designed by Tom Doak and opened in 2024, the course features dramatic elevation changes and a landscape that is unique among the resort's ten courses.

Few golf course projects had more national attention in recent years than Quail Hollow, mainly because of its role as a regular PGA Tour stop and its hosting of the 2017 PGA Championship and 2022 Presidents Cup. The course has been extensively modified by Tom Fazio over the years.

I played Eagle Point Golf Club, a Tom Fazio design, soon after it became ranked on Golf Digest's 100 Greatest. Eagle Point is a beautifully rolling, pine-lined Augusta National clone with huge immaculate greens and a first-class private experience.

Unlike nearby Wade Hampton Golf Club, the Tom Fazio design that was routed through natural valleys, Mountaintop sits on more rugged, mountainous terrain. Opened in 2006, the course is known for its impeccable conditioning and stunning long-range views.

While architecture purists scoff at the notion of waterfalls on golf courses, there is something magical about the Tom Fazio design at Diamond Creek. Opened in 2003, the course is set at a high elevation and features some of the most dramatic terrain in the state.

Created by architect Perry Maxwell on the heels of his work at Prairie Dunes and Southern Hills, Old Town Club opened in 1939. A 2013 restoration by Bill Coore and Ben Crenshaw removed hundreds of trees and restored the wide corridors and massive green complexes.

In 2010, a team led by Bill Coore and Ben Crenshaw killed and ripped out all the Bermudagrass rough at Donald Ross's masterpiece. In its place, they restored the sandy waste areas and wiregrass that were present when the course hosted the 1951 Ryder Cup.

Built during the period when Tom Fazio was still working with the existing landscape rather than building it, Wade Hampton (1987) is a masterclass in mountain golf architecture. It has been a mainstay near the top of our rankings for decades.

The Sunset course at Las Campanas is located in the high desert of Santa Fe and offers breathtaking views of the surrounding Sangre de Cristo Mountain range, along with other mountain ranges in the far distance. Jack Nicklaus did an excellent job of incorporating these features into his target lines off the tees. The course presents some blind shots but overall provides ample room off the tee.
courses.other_details: 3 PANELISTS, Previous rank: 1, High desert setting, Sangre de Cristo Mountain views.
courses.course_name: The Club At Las Campanas: Sunset

courses.other_details: 8 PANELISTS, Previous rank: 2, 6,500-foot elevation, Best vista in New Mexico at the par-4 17th.
courses.description: Paako Ridge, Golf Digest's Best New Affordable Public Course of 2000, is quite long from the tips and regular tees. A golf ball carries farther at 6,500-foot elevation, but Paako also plays long because both nines work up mountain foothills for several holes before playing downhill. The 496-yard par-4 seventh is the same shape and dimensions as the 10th at Augusta National.

Black Mesa flows through a Martian landscape of buttes, sagebrush-dotted ridges and dry arroyos. The Baxter Spann design feels worlds away, with shots through canyons, along bluffs and over rock outcroppings to blind targets. There is no development on or near the vast, high-desert property.

The first of two Jack Nicklaus courses at Las Campanas—his 100th course in his portfolio—was built in 1993 and set within the foothills of Santa Fe, sitting at about 7,000 feet above sea level with numerous elevation changes and a good mix of uphill and downhill holes. Most tee shots provide ample room, but precision into the greens and par-5s is challenging.
courses.course_name: The Club At Las Campanas: Sunrise

courses.description: Twin Warriors is on the grounds of the Santa Ana Pueblo and has 20 cultural sites, including an ancient cave dwelling on the 15th hole. Set in the high desert, the course has beautiful native features, including dry washes, eroded land and a butte that is ever present on the course.

Located in the high desert of northern New Mexico, the course fits the soft natural contours of the existing land as fairways glide over gentle slopes and offer stunning views of the surrounding canyons. The layout includes risk-reward opportunities with snaking waste areas, small creeks, and ponds.
Sandia Golf Club in Albuquerque is one of the best courses in New Mexico. Discover our experts' reviews and where Sandia ranks in our rankings.

The Ken Dye-designed Red Hawk Golf Club sits at the base of the Organ Mountains and features a unique mix of links and prairie-style golf. The fairways and greens are set over harsh, undulating terrain, making flat lies and straight putts rare. Large sloped putting surfaces often feature multiple tiers.
courses.other_details: Previous rank: NR, Links and prairie-style golf, Organ Mountains views.

courses.description: The Red Lawrence-designed championship course at the University of New Mexico debuted in 1967 and is one of the starkest tests of golf in the region. Host of the 1988 NCAA Championships, the course is constructed over steep, high desert terrain with undulating fairways guarded by waste areas and penal greenside bunkering.
courses.other_details: 5 PANELISTS, Previous rank: NR, Host of 1988 NCAA Championships.
courses.course_name: Championship Golf Course At University of New Mexico

courses.architect: Donald Ross (1913), renovations by Robert Trent Jones (1952) and Mike DeVries (2007)
courses.other_details: Built on a tight 110 acres. Previous rank: NR.
courses.description: Siwanoy Country Club, just a few miles up the Hutchinson River Parkway from New York City, was originally founded in 1901 and debuted its 18-hole Donald Ross-designed golf course in 1913. The course has retained nearly all of its original Ross characteristics even after renovations by Robert Trent Jones in 1952 and Mike DeVries in 2007. Tree removal during the most recent renovation has allowed the course to return to its original design, with more open driving areas and expanded putting surfaces. Built on a tight 110 acres and measuring just under 6,700 yards, Siwanoy's challenge comes in the form of firm and immaculate greens with gigantic slopes and extensive run-offs.

courses.course_name: Montauk Downs State Park Golf Course
courses.description: Situated about a mile from the Atlantic Ocean on the tip of Long Island, Montauk Downs is a challenging layout.
This is Pete Dye at his prettiest—and toughest. Pound Ridge, which sits 40 miles northeast of Manhattan, is a scenic yet challenging public layout.
In the era of social media, it’s tough to remain a true hidden gem these days. If it’s a good course, people will find it.
Located in the Hamptons just off of Noyac Bay sits the William Mitchell-designed Noyac Golf Club.
Less than 45 minutes north of Albany, Saratoga National is a scenic yet challenging public layout.
Located to the east of Buffalo and originally founded in 1903, Park Country Club of Buffalo debuted its current layout in the early 20th century.
Old Westbury Golf and Country Club debuted its golf course in 1960, commissioning William Mitchell to design the original layout.
courses.course_name: Old Westbury Golf & Country Club: Overlook/Woods/Blue Grass

courses.description: Just down the road from the National Baseball Hall of Fame in Cooperstown, The Otesaga’s Leatherstocking Golf Course is a historic public course.
Like Turning Stone’s other two 18-hole tracks, the Shenendoah course is a past member of our 100 Greatest Public Courses.
In a neighborhood of fabulous Golden Age designs, Metropolis Country Club might be overlooked—but it shouldn't be.
Nassau Country Club was founded in 1896 and has since been expanded and renovated by a host of famous architects.
Westchester Country Club, located in Rye, N.Y., debuted in 1922, commissioning Walter Travis to design its courses.
Manhattan Woods Golf Club opened in 1998 and is set among densely forested hills overlooking the Hudson River.
Country Club of Troy is situated on rocky tumbling hills above the Hudson River.
Westchester County golf is full of hidden gems, but Sunningdale Country Club is often overlooked.
Located in the hills overlooking the Long Island Sound, just 25 miles from downtown New York City.
The Country Club of Rochester was founded in 1895, and Donald Ross’ design opened in 1913.
Old Oaks Country Club, located in Purchase, N.Y., has a unique architectural pedigree—boasting an A.W. Tillinghast design.
Located in Roslyn Harbor along the north shore of Long Island is the Herbert Strong-designed Engineers Country Club.
Just like Turning Stone’s Atunyote course, the Kaluhyat layout was also previously ranked.

courses.course_name: Bally's Golf Links at Ferry Point
courses.architect: Jack Nicklaus, John Stanford and John Sanford
courses.description: Bally's Golf Links Ferry Point, formerly Trump Ferry Point, is a Jack Nicklaus, John Stanford and John Sanford design.
Dustin Johnson and Matt Kuchar each captured PGA Tour titles at Turning Stone’s Atunyote course.
East Hampton is a unique Bill Coore and Ben Crenshaw design that occupies a little over 120 acres.
courses.other_details: Occupies a little over 120 acres. Previous rank: 31.

courses.description: The Robert Trent Jones-designed golf course at Crag Burn Golf Club opened in 1972.
The Rockaway Hunting Club in Lawrence is one of the best courses in New York.
Located on the edges of Shinnecock Hills and the National Golf Links of America is a Seth Raynor-designed course.
Monroe Golf Club premiered in 1924 with a spectacular Donald Ross design routed as two distinct nines.
Found on an incredibly undulating and varied piece of land, Whippoorwill Club is one of the finest in the area.
Steeped in history dating back to 1898 and two other previous sites.
Fenway is a fine A.W. Tillinghast design that often gets overlooked because of its famous neighbors.
At a certain point in their past, specifically the early 1950s, the roads of Oak Hill’s two courses were rerouted.
The Country Club of Buffalo was originally founded in 1894 and played host to the 1912 U.S. Open.
Meadow Brook Club in Jericho is one of the best golf courses in New York.
Originally designed by C.B. Macdonald and Seth Raynor in 1924.
Silo Ridge Field Club is yet another excellent Tom Fazio layout built for Mike Meldman’s Discovery Land Co.
Westhampton Country Club flies under the radar in the golf-rich mecca of the Hamptons.
Well-known as a venue that hosted a PGA Tour event for decades.
Wykagyl is routed on one of the most dramatic pieces of land in Westchester County.
Glens Falls Country Club is one of the best golf courses in New York. Discover our experts' reviews.

courses.course_name: St. George's Golf and Country Club
courses.description: St. George's Golf and Country Club in East Setauket is one of the best golf courses in New York.
The 27-hole facility at Glen Oaks was originally designed by Joe Finger in 1971.
The Bridge, which opened in 2002, is one of the newest clubs in the Hamptons and has a high initiation fee.
Real-estate developer Lowell Schulman hired Rees Jones to create his dream golf club on rolling linksland.
When it was conceived in the early 1920s, The Creek was considered “The Million Dollar Club”.
C.B. Macdonald designed Piping Rock right after he completed National Golf Links.
Hudson National rests on dramatic bluffs high over the Hudson River valley.
Quaker Ridge returned to our list of America’s 100 Greatest Courses in 2013 thanks to major revisions.
Minimalist in its design (you can still see the faint traces of old roadbeds over which the course was built).
Not only one of America’s earliest links courses, Maidstone is also one of the country’s earliest great clubs.
In the mid-2000s, the late George Bahto, who had extensively researched the works of Macdonald and Raynor, renovated the course.
Not since Augusta National had the nation’s greatest golfer (Nicklaus) teamed with one of its most outspoken architects (Doak).
Winged Foot’s two-course complex is the product of A.W. Tillinghast’s fertile imagination. Every hole is a challenge.
Sprawling Bethpage Black, designed in the mid-1930s to be “the public Pine Valley,” became the darling of US Opens.
Back in 1979, George Fazio and nephew Tom were roundly criticized for removing Ross characteristics, but it remains top-tier.
The challenge for architects Bill Coore and Ben Crenshaw at Friar’s Head was to design holes in both dunes and farm fields.
Gone are all the Norway Spruce that once squeezed every fairway of Winged Foot West. It’s now gloriously open.
Undoubtedly, Fishers Island Club is the most dazzling design by architect Seth Raynor, who died in 1926.
This is where golf architect Seth Raynor got his start, surveying for C.B. Macdonald.
Generally considered to be the earliest links in America, heavily remodeled by C.B. Macdonald, then William Flynn.
The Links of North Dakota in Ray is one of the best courses in North Dakota. Discover our experts’ reviews and where The Links of North Dakota Golf Club sits in our rankings.
Fargo Country Club is one of the best golf courses in North Dakota. Discover our experts' reviews and course information.
Bully Pulpit Golf Course in Medora is one of the best courses in North Dakota. Discover our experts’ reviews and where Bully Pulpit Golf Course sits in our rankings.
Oxbow Country Club in Oxbow is one of the best courses in North Dakota. Discover our experts’ reviews and where Oxbow Country Club sits in our rankings.
Hawktree Golf Club in Bismark is one of the best courses in North Dakota. Discover our experts' reviews and where Hawktree Golf Club sits in our rankings.
The Minot Country Club in Minot is one of the best courses in North Dakota. Discover our experts’ reviews and where The Minot Golf Club sits in our rankings.

Although it didn't get built for another 15 years, Fallen Oak was first conceived in the early 1990s by Las Vegas casino mogul Steve Wynn soon after Tom Fazio had completed Shadow Creek. Wynn wanted Fazio to design a similar course for his Beau Rivage casino hotel on the Mississippi Gulf Coast. Unlike Shadow Creek, it's built on rolling forest and wetlands, with no need for mammoth earth-moving.

Developed by the George Bryan family and Toxey Haas, Mossy Oak is known as 'Nature's Golf'. Hanse got the job before he was awarded the Rio Olympics design in 2012, and it was the first project he tackled after completing his work in Brazil. The site footprint is smaller than Old Waverly across the road, but the course feels more expansive with no residential areas on it.

Founder George Bryan, chairman of the meat division of Sara Lee Corporation, created Old Waverly in tiny West Point, MS. It was the first collaboration between Jerry Pate and Bob Cupp. The course hosted the 1999 U.S. Women's Open.
Situated on the former site of the Annandale Plantation, Annandale Golf Club was founded in 1981 by local business leaders. Jack Nicklaus surveyed the property when it was a rural soybean field.

Part of the Pearl River Resort, Azaleas was the first of two courses at Dancing Rabbit. It features Bermuda fairways and A-4 Bentgrass greens, providing a distinct agronomic profile from its sister course, the Oaks.
A Jack Nicklaus Signature Design located in the DeSoto National Forest. It wraps around wetlands and natural areas, offering a secluded and natural golf experience.

Recognized by Audubon International, this Jerry Pate design makes compelling use of the natural terrain with native fescues, bunkers, and marshlands. It requires quality ball-striking off the tee due to forced carries over wetlands.
The second course at Dancing Rabbit Golf Club, opening two years after the Azaleas. It features Zoysia fairways and Bermuda greens, and is known for its grand old oak trees and challenging layout.
courses.course_name: Dancing Rabbit Golf Club: The Oaks

courses.description: A Davis Love III design that roams through local wetlands and rolling marshlands. It features generous fairways guarded by large bunkers and vast, undulating greens. The course was recognized as one of the best new upscale public courses in America when it opened.

When Bobby Jones won the 1930 U.S. Open at Interlachen (completing the second leg of what would become the game's first Grand Slam), fellow competitor Gene Sarazen insisted the course was tougher than everything but Oakmont. In the decades that followed a series of architects including Robert Trent Jones, Geoffrey Cornish and Brian Silva worked to keep Interlachen's edge, but nothing could staunch the march of time that made the course one-dimensional through the shrinkage of greens and the maturation of the hundreds of trees that had been planted, shading fairways and masking the property's natural land movements. Enter Andrew Green in 2023, who was given the resources to strip back the layers and rebuild the course based on the blueprints Donald Ross developed in 1922 when he remodeled the course. Interlachen's ediness is back, with ominous, strategically arranged bunkers guarding greens and fairway lines, and the expanded putting surfaces presenting a range of come-and-get-me hole locations that haven't been seen in ages. The restored bunkering shines a spotlight on Interlachen's wondrous undulation, punctuating focal points like the shared promontory of the second and seventh greens and the majestic rise toward the fortress putting surface of the par-5 12th.

While Tom Fazio is best known for creating massive landscapes for his designs, Spring Hill required little manipulation of earth. Fazio utilized the existing rolling topography to form what is one of his most natural designs. Holes are isolated from one another by thick forests of evergreens and, in one section of the property, acres of maple trees that provide a brilliant color display each fall. With several tight fairways, marshland along some edges, many uphill approach shots into elevated greens and subtle movements in the putting surfaces, Spring Hill is also one of Fazio's most challenging designs.

Hazeltine might be the most controversial championship course of the modern era, designed by Robert Trent Jones for former USGA president Totton Heffelfinger, who used his considerable clout to bring the 1966 U.S. Women's Open and 1970 U.S. Open to the then-very immature layout. Criticisms were so extreme that Trent Jones spent the next two decades remodeling it, straightening doglegs, relocating holes and rebuilding greens. Between 1987 and 2010, his younger son, Rees Jones, assumed the reconstruction, with even greater success—and today the layout, like many in the old man’s portfolio, is more Rees than Trent. Hazeltine hosted the 2009 PGA and 2016 Ryder Cup, the latter a bright spot for the American team, which perhaps is why the PGA of America has already awarded the 2028 Ryder Cup to this Minnesota site. Davis Love III, longtime Ryder Cup player and victorious U.S. captain in 2016, will step in for Rees and make modifications to the course in preparation with his brother Mark and their lead architect, Scot Sherman.

Before he moved to California, where he laid the foundation of many of that state's best courses from the pre-Depression era, William Watson was a pioneer of golf in Minnesota. He arranged the first nine holes at White Bear Yacht Club in 1912 near the shore of White Bear Lake on some of the most roly-poly land imaginable. Several years later, Watson added another nine holes and proceeded to remodel the entire course. Donald Ross has long been rumored to have done the remodel work, but the club doesn't have evidence of this and is now of the mind that the course is entirely Watson's creation. The site's wildly rumpled, unmodified land is the heart and soul of White Bear Yacht Club. Modern architects would likely have leveled and softened the slopes and ravines, but here they bring the golf to life visually and psychologically, offering nary a level stance and asking the player to drive to high sides of the tilted fairways and hit approaches with extreme control. Over the last two decades, under the guidance of Jim Urbina, the surrounding canopy of forest has been pared back to better reveal the massive, enthralling undulations of the course, and several holes, including the par-3s at six and 11, as well as the 12th and 18th green complex, are currently being restored.

Something was in the air in Minneapolis and St. Paul in the 1920s. Call it an esprit de refaire (French for "repair"). Many of the prominent clubs in the market looked at their existing golf courses and thought, "we can do better." The Twin Cities were an incubator for the first major movement of golf course reformation as courses laid out just years before by designers like Tom Bendelow, William Watson and Willie Park, Jr., were redesigned, most of them by Donald Ross. Interlachen (No. 1), White Bear Yacht Club (No. 4), The Club at Golden Valley (No. 15), Minneapolis G.C. and others. The Minikahda Club also falls into this category. Robert Foulis designed the original nine, and Watson was involved in some capacity, too. Then the club hired Donald Ross to completely revamp the course after Chick Evans dismantled the old design in the 1916 U.S. Open, shooting a then-record 286 using just seven clubs. Many more renovations ensued, but the club has perhaps settled on a lasting version after the work of Kyle Franz, who continued a program of thinning overgrowth, widening fairways and recreating bunker shapes and arrangements reminiscent of the club's 1920s heyday.

It doesn't get the press that courses such as Bandon Dunes, Pacific Dunes, Whistling Straits or Arcadia Bluffs, but The Quarry at Giants Ridge plays very links-like with its collection of fairway speed slots, greenside backboards and backstops and reverse-camber greens. It's very inventive design also demands some aerial play, too. A standout is its 13th, a drivable par-4 that's nearly as wide as it is long, with three alternate routes to a 100-yard-wide green. We named it the best 13th hole in America built since 2000.

John Fought and Minnesota native Tom Lehman were given 243 acres of rolling pastureland 25 miles west of Minneapolis to build Windsong Farm's South Course. Opened in 2003, this golfers' club is designed to test the best players with a big ballpark of a course tipping out at 7,380 yards. Originally, the fescue-lined playing corridors were relatively tight. Fought returned and widened the fairways, which opened up different lines to drive and better angles into the greens. The collection areas around the greens allow for varying kinds of recovery shots back to the large, moderately sloping greens. While it is still a challenging golf course, it is very playable and a walker's paradise. The course's second 18, the North, also designed by Fought, opened in the summer of 2024.

In 2005, The Wilderness at Fortune Bay won America's Best New Upscale Public Course, a year after architect Jeff Brauer won the same award for The Quarry at Giants Ridge, also in northern Minnesota. Where The Quarry uses slopes and ramps, Wilderness rewards aerial play, with some high-low alternate fairways, lake-edged greens and a pair of drop-shot par-3s. As we wrote back in 2005, "its options outnumber its rock outcroppings, and there are outcroppings galore."
Somerset Country Club in Mendota Heights is one of the best courses in Minnesota. Discover our experts’ reviews and where Somerset Country Club sits in our rankings.

The Classic is a genuine amateur architect design, although course superintendent Scott Hoffmann consulted with veteran course architect Geoff Cornish as well as others in creating The Classic at Madden's. It's beautiful but not for the faint of heart, a hilly course with some narrow, pine-lined fairways and occasional challenging shots over water from sidehill or downhill lies. But, like other multiple-course operations, such as Bethpage and Cog Hill, Madden's has easier alternate layouts for high-handicappers.
courses.course_name: Madden's on Gull Lake: The Classic at Madden's

courses.description: Architect Ron Prichard took an existing layout by Charles Maddox and completely transformed it—with a brand-new course reopening in 2015 that is one of the best in Minnesota. Tyler Rae has done some renovation work in recent years to clear out a significant number of trees to open up sightlines and tweak green complexes to make some less severe. Still, Olympic Hills features intriguing greensites built in all shapes and sizes, with a good variety of holes.

Northwood sits on an elevated site with arresting views of Lake Superior. Built in 1921, the steep terrain was baffling to route and challenging to build on in the era before most construction jobs used modern earth-moving equipment. Ross solved the puzzle by layering the holes up and down the slopes like switchback roads, forcing him to do things he ordinarily wouldn’t. The third hole climbs some 100 feet from tee to green, while the 10th plays across the pitched hill. Keeping the tee ball in the fairway is, at best, awkward. The greens do not have much interior contour, but the tilted surfaces require expert surveying skills. In recent years, some judicious tree clearing and a change in maintenance have resulted in firm and fast playing surfaces.

Midland Hills always suspected their course was designed by Seth Raynor in the early 1920s, but they had no records of what that course looked like. They could intuit what some of Raynor's original holes were, but the overall architecture had dulled and shrunk over the years. That changed in 2018 when superintendent Mike Manthey discovered a 1921 irrigation map hidden above the ceiling in his office. The drawing showed Raynor's vision for the course, including individual holes and bunkers, a roadmap that designer Jim Urbina used to recreate and sharpen the old templates like the Biarritz, Road and Eden. The improvements have helped vault the course into the Best in State rankings for the first time.

In 1920, Donald Ross was invited to remodel this 1916 Willie Park Jr. design. For some unknown reason, Ross did not include Minneapolis GC in his resume. His work here wasn't insignificant: Ross moved the clubhouse and reconfigured the routing, making it mostly his own, and the course was deemed strong enough to host the 1959 PGA Championship, won by Bob Rosburg. A recent renovation by Jeff Mingay altered the bunkering and recaptured many original green contours. It is well known in the Twin Cities as the "Player’s Club" because of the number of single-digit handicappers.

For most of its history, Golden Valley has been in a state of flux. Tom Bendelow designed the club's first course before A.W. Tillinghast was hired in the 1920s to construct a new course. A number of his bunkers were removed in the 1930s to reduce maintenance costs (Tillinghast, by this time, was traveling the country on behalf of the PGA of America, advising clubs on ways to cut costs during tight economic times), and others were tinkered with and modified in one way or another. A series of mid-century renovations took the design further from the one Tillinghast put in the ground, but recent work, first by Ron Forse and, in 2023, by Kevin Norby, has gradually restored much of the architect's ideology. Greens have been expanded to add new hole locations, trees have been thinned and Tillie's lost bunkers have returned with their shapes redefined. This is a classic parkland expression of golf on a graceful property that demands drives be placed in the fairway and approach shots left below the hole.

The design lineage of Minnesota Valley is cloudy, but the club believes the Seth Raynor drafted plans for the course during one of his visits to Minneapolis in the early 1920s (it’s doubtful he was involved in the actual construction of the course). But the design if full of Raynor and C.B. Macdonald ideas, and no matter who gets original credit, Minnesota Valley now fully looks and plays like a Raynor course following a 2018 renovation by Bill Bergin that enhanced the character of holes like Short, Redan, Raynor’s Prize Dogleg and a Biarritz green at a par-4 rather than the usual par-3.

A former member of our 100 Greatest Public list, Deacon’s Lodge is a scenic Arnold Palmer signature course that plays through woodlands and around lakes. The fairways have a lot of movement to them and are often banked on the sides, filtering balls toward the middle. There are some forced carries over native grasses, which add to the aesthetics of this central Minnesota layout.

Created by Texas-based architect Jeff Brauer, The Legends course at this 36-hole public complex in upper Minnesota has always played second-fiddle in our rankings to the more highly regarded Quarry Course. Located two-and-a-half miles north of its brother, it's nevertheless an ideal sibling with holes similarly gouged from a wilderness of forest and crackled ground movement. You can get the sense of being lost in time and forest journeying through the vast areas the course traverses.

courses.description: Like Bandon Dunes and Sand Hills before it, Sutton Bay is a continuation in the modern trend of great golf courses being built in remote locations. Situated 40 miles northwest of Pierre, S.D., Sutton Bay was created by South Dakota native Mark Amundson, who wanted to create a retreat with golf, hunting and fishing. He found his spot on a piece of land on Lake Oahe, perfect for all three. Amundson hired Graham Marsh, who wrote in the course’s yardage book, “The brief for the golf course was simple. The course should be playable yet challenging, keep earthworks to a minimum, and preserve the natural landscape.” Marsh certainly achieved his goal, the course feels placed within—not on top of the land, with unique landforms, sprawling bunker complexes and beautiful views, the course well earns the top spot in South Dakota.

courses.description: While a lot of golf in Vermont conjures tight, tree-lined holes snaking through mountainous terrain, Ekwanok Country Club is a more expansive setting in a gently rolling valley at the base of Mt. Equinox. The generous fairways framed by fescue encourage a lot of drivers, and the main defense against scoring are greens with ample interior movement. The conditioning is usually near impeccable, and when they’re fast, they command full attention. The par-5 seventh hole, which requires a blind second shot over a hill, has never been reached in two and might be the most famous hole in Vermont.
The Country Club of Vermont is newer (it opened in 1998), but the property feels secluded and old.

courses.description: For years, Rutland Country Club has been known throughout the state for its greens. Small, quick and true, they defend the course against its modest yardage. Originally a nine-hole design set on farmland, it was converted into an 18-hole course in 1927 by Stiles and Van Kleek. The back nine is built into the hillside, demanding well-placed tee balls and confident approaches into several elevated greens, all while delivering striking mountain views.

Founded in 1886, boasts itself to be the oldest continuously operated golf club in the United States. For years, it was a nine-hole course, but in 1999, the major project to make the course 18 holes was completed by Vermont designer Steve Durkee. Don’t let the yardage on the card fool you, Dorset is plenty of a test. Several tight landing areas, beautiful yet penal fescue and quick, undulating greens that keep you thinking are a few features that will make you forget the course is 6,240 yards. No. 16 features a 90-degree dogleg and a green surrounded by picturesque birches.

courses.description: Built in a 250-acre meadow beneath the Grand Teton Mountains and its ski runs, Shooting Star is a core-golf layout with no housing. Its opening nine runs counterclockwise around the perimeter of the site, and its incoming nine circles clockwise through the interior. The flat land was re-sculpted into hills and valleys, then lightly planted with aspens and evergreens. Water hazards, in the form of 50 acres of lakes, ponds and a canal reshaped into a stream, affect play on 13 of the 18 holes. The club displays green sketches of all 18 in the clubhouse, each signed by Fazio, and the notations on them reflect some of Fazio’s design instructions.

Just south of Jackson Hole, the Snake River Sporting Club is a four-season resort and residential community with a Tom Weiskopf-designed course that embraces the natural contours of the river valley. Set beside the 1,078-mile-long Snake River and surrounded by mountains, the scenic course winds through tall cottonwoods and sagebrush. The signature par-3 15th is nearly an island green, surrounded not by water but by native grasses, which beautifully frame the hole against the backdrop of the Snake River.

Located in western Wyoming, Jackson Hole Golf & Tennis Club is a semi-private Robert Trent Jones Jr. design. Near both Grand Teton and Yellowstone National Parks, the course offers stunning views of the surrounding mountains and wildlife. The relatively flat course is framed nicely by tall, native grass and stately trees. The scenic layout hosted the 1988 U.S. Amateur Public Links and the 1993 U.S. Women’s Amateur Public Links.

courses.description: Devils Tower is the tale of two courses. The first nine, built in the 1990s, wander an open prairie section of the property. In 2005, veteran Colorado-based architect Dick Phelps and then-associate Kevin Atkinson remodeled the existing holes and added nine more pulse-racing holes on more dynamic land to the south. These play through ponderosa woodlands and skirt the edge of a high canyon wall overlooking the Belle Fourche River, and when not in the trees, offer views of Devils Tower National Monument over eight miles in the distance.

In 2000, mining company officers John Raese and Bob Gwynne started building a golf course on a newly acquired parcel of forest that their firm will eventually mine for limestone. Using company engineers and construction equipment, and guidance by veteran tour pros Johnny Pott and Dow Finsterwald, they spent almost a decade creating Pikewood National. A natural waterfall became the backdrop for their par-3 fifth hole and the linchpin of their routing, which plays along bluffs, through forest over rapids, and on the hook-shaped par-5 eighth, around a gulch.

Like No. 31 Pikewood National, Pete Dye Golf Club is a 100 Greatest course built atop a West Virginia mining deposit, but in this case, it’s an active coal mine. Construction took so long that Dye’s style had evolved by the time the full 18 was finally seeded. Early holes sport the railroad-tie motif of Oak Tree National, while later holes feature the echo-chamber bunkering of Whistling Straits. There’s even a coal mine shaft that leads to the seventh tee.
